title: Welcome to JavaScript
instruction:
You just picked the most widely used programming language in the world.

JavaScript runs in every browser, every web app, every Node.js server. Instagram, Netflix, Airbnb — all JavaScript. And it is shockingly quick to pick up.

`console.log()` is how JavaScript talks to you. Whatever you put inside the parentheses gets printed.

Hit **Run** and see what happens.
successMessage: Did you see that? One line of code, instant output. That is JavaScript. Welcome to the most-used language on the planet.

title: Make it say your name
instruction:
Change the message to greet yourself.

JavaScript strings can use double quotes `"` or single quotes `'` — both work exactly the same. Pick a style and stick to it.

Change the text to `Hello, [your name]!` — put your actual name in there.
hint: Just change the text inside the quotes. Keep the quotes themselves.

codeChecks.message: Change the message — make it say hello to you!

successMessage: Your name on the screen. This program belongs to you now. That warm feeling? Programmers call it 'it works'. Get very used to it.

title: Print multiple things
instruction:
Each `console.log()` prints on its own line. Run as many as you want — JavaScript goes top to bottom, one at a time.

Add a second and third `console.log()` below the first. Tell it something about you.
hint:
console.log("I am learning JavaScript.");
console.log("This is my third line.");
starter:
console.log("Hello, Alex!");
// Add two more console.log() calls below

codeChecks.message: Add two more console.log() calls — three total.

successMessage: Three lines! JavaScript ran them in order, top to bottom. Every program you will ever write follows this same rule — instructions execute in sequence.

title: The semicolon situation
instruction:
JavaScript semicolons are optional — the language has Automatic Semicolon Insertion (ASI) that adds them for you.

But most style guides (and your future teammates) expect them. In this course we use semicolons consistently.

Run this — both versions work. Then add one more line without a semicolon to confirm it still runs.
successMessage: Both work. JavaScript is forgiving about semicolons. We use them anyway — they make intent clear and prevent edge-case bugs where ASI guesses wrong.

title: Comments — notes for humans
instruction:
Comments are completely ignored by JavaScript. They are notes for the humans reading the code.

- `//` — single-line comment (everything after it on that line)
- `/* ... */` — multi-line comment block

Run this. Notice neither comment appears in the output. Then change the `//` comment to describe what the program does.
hint: Change the // comment text. The output won't change.
successMessage: Comments never appear in output. Good comments explain WHY the code does something — not what (the code shows that). Future you will be very grateful for them.

title: typeof — what kind of thing is this?
instruction:
`typeof` tells you the data type of any value:

```js
typeof "hello"  // 'string'
typeof 42       // 'number'
typeof true     // 'boolean'
typeof undefined // 'undefined'
```

Run this. You will see all five types. Later you will understand why `typeof null === 'object'` is one of JavaScript's most famous quirks.
successMessage: typeof null === 'object' is a 25-year-old bug in JavaScript that can never be fixed because too much code depends on it. Knowing this saves you from future confusion.

title: Spot the bug — missing quote
instruction:
This program crashes. A string is not closed properly — a quote is missing.

Find it and fix it before running.
hint: Look at line 1. The closing quote is missing.

codeChecks.message: Fix the missing closing quote on line 1.

successMessage: Syntax error fixed! Unclosed strings are the most common crash for beginners. Your editor will usually highlight them in red — learn to read those hints.

title: Case sensitivity
instruction:
JavaScript is case-sensitive. `Console.log` is not the same as `console.log`.

`console.log` works. `Console.Log` crashes.

Fix both broken lines.

codeChecks.message: Use lowercase: console.log — JavaScript is case-sensitive!

successMessage: console.log — always lowercase. After a few hundred calls your fingers type it automatically. Promise.

title: Numbers and arithmetic
instruction:
JavaScript handles math directly in `console.log` — no extra steps:

```js
console.log(5 + 3);   // 8
console.log(10 - 4);  // 6
console.log(3 * 7);   // 21
console.log(15 / 4);  // 3.75
console.log(15 % 4);  // 3  (remainder)
console.log(2 ** 10); // 1024 (power)
```

Print the result of all six operations.
hint: Six console.log calls, one per operation.
starter: // TODO: print results of all 6 arithmetic operations

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: All six operations! % (modulo/remainder) is used constantly in programming — it tells you if a number is even, creates wrap-around counters, and appears in FizzBuzz.

title: Write it from scratch
instruction:
No starter code. Blank page. You write the whole thing.

Print exactly:
```
JavaScript is awesome.
I built this from scratch.
Chapter 1 complete!
```

Three `console.log()` calls. Each exact string on its own line.
hint: Three console.log calls, one per line. Match the text exactly.

codeChecks.message: Use console.log() to print each line.

successMessage: Built from nothing. Three lines, three outputs, zero guidance. That is what programming is — blank screen to working code. Chapter 1 done!
